narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 69.9% chance that you would not pass—well above the threshold we use for admissions decisions. This prediction draws from patterns in data from thousands of past students, focusing on academic and background factors like grades, test scores, and family circumstances, while setting aside demographics like gender or race. In your case, the model zeroed in on a few key aspects of your profile that shaped this outcome. The strongest influence pushing toward a failure prediction was your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1, which is notably lower than the average of about 6 for students who passed the bar—suggesting your later undergraduate performance raised concerns about sustaining the rigor needed for law school and the exam. Next, your Year 1 decile of 1 similarly contributed to the risk assessment, again falling far short of the typical 6 seen among successful bar takers, indicating early academic challenges that the model views as persistent hurdles. On a more positive note, your LSAT score of 37 worked in your favor, nearly matching the 37.27 average for those who passed and pulling the prediction somewhat toward success. Other elements, like your middle family income bracket—which aligns with about a third of bar passers—and your plan to attend full-time, which is common among successful students, played supporting roles but weren't decisive enough to shift the overall outlook. Compared to admitted applicants, who typically show stronger grade deciles and balanced profiles, your lower rankings in those early and later years stood out as the biggest departures, making your predicted path riskier in the model's eyes. This isn't a judgment on your potential or drive, but a data-driven flag based on historical trends. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that strength even higher, or focusing on graduate-level courses to improve your academic deciles and demonstrate upward momentum. Building a stronger narrative around overcoming those early hurdles through recommendations or personal essays could also help in reapplying. Many students in similar spots have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps—reach out to admissions for targeted advice on bolstering your next application. With persistence, you can reshape this story.
